coredata.dc:title: TiO2/CDs modified thin-film nanocomposite polyamide membrane for simultaneous enhancement of antifouling and chlorine-resistance performance
coredata.dc:creator: Vatanpour, Vahid
Paziresh, Shadi
Naziri Mehrabani, Seyed Ali
Feizpoor, Solmaz
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Koyuncu, Ismail
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a titanium dioxide/carbon dots (TiO2/CDs) hydrophilic nanocomposite photocatalyst with different contents was embedded in the polyamide layer of reverse osmosis (RO) membrane via interfacial polymerization to increase desalination performance and antifouling properties. The modified membranes showed smoother surfaces with reduced contact angles, causing to improve membrane fouling resistance. TiO2/CDs nanocomposite, due to its photocatalytic activity, showed more flux recovery ratio (FRR) after irradiation of UV light during the washing process. The FRR was enhanced from 94.4 to 97.1% for 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs RO membrane by UV irradiation. The hydrophilic functional groups with negative charge increased the desalination efficiency of the modified membranes (99.2% NaCl rejection for 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs membrane). The membrane containing 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs presented the highest pure water and NaCl solution flux of (59.6 and 54.6 L·m−2·h−1) at 15 bar, respectively. In addition, the used nanoparticles improved the chlorine resistance of the membranes. As a result, TiO2/CDs nanocomposite with little amounts could be applied as a suitable nanofiller to increase the permeability, antifouling properties, and chlorine resistance for RO membrane modification.

head.ce:title: TiO2/CDs modified thin-film nanocomposite polyamide membrane for simultaneous enhancement of antifouling and chlorine-resistance performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a titanium dioxide/carbon dots (TiO2/CDs) hydrophilic nanocomposite photocatalyst with different contents was embedded in the polyamide layer of reverse osmosis (RO) membrane via interfacial polymerization to increase desalination performance and antifouling properties. The modified membranes showed smoother surfaces with reduced contact angles, causing to improve membrane fouling resistance. TiO2/CDs nanocomposite, due to its photocatalytic activity, showed more flux recovery ratio (FRR) after irradiation of UV light during the washing process. The FRR was enhanced from 94.4 to 97.1% for 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs RO membrane by UV irradiation. The hydrophilic functional groups with negative charge increased the desalination efficiency of the modified membranes (99.2% NaCl rejection for 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs membrane). The membrane containing 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs presented the highest pure water and NaCl solution flux of (59.6 and 54.6 L·m−2·h−1) at 15 bar, respectively. In addition, the used nanoparticles improved the chlorine resistance of the membranes. As a result, TiO2/CDs nanocomposite with little amounts could be applied as a suitable nanofiller to increase the permeability, antifouling properties, and chlorine resistance for RO membrane modification.

ce:section.ce:para: As time elapses, the water famine phenomenon arising from industrialization, climate fluctuations, contamination, and population increment, is an imperative consideration of humankind. Water treatment and reuse technologies have been emerged to overcome water scarcity. Desalination, as a conventional and mainly used technology, is attributed to the removal of salt, harmful ions, and other contaminants from the seawater, which can be classified into phase-change (thermal and crystallization) and membrane-based desalination in terms of mechanism of separation [1–4].

Reverse osmosis (RO) desalination is a highly preferred and effective method for wastewater reuse and replenishment of water resources compared to a nanofiltration (NF) and electrodialysis, thanks to the simplicity, low operational cost, energy efficiency, and high-quality water production [5]. Thin-film composite membranes (TFC) are the most used and common membranes for the RO process. Polyamide TFC membranes are advantageous due to their independent control for obtaining desired permeability and rejection, high-temperature tolerance, usability in a wide pH range, resistance to high pressure, and cost efficiency [6,7].

Besides the aforementioned features of TFC membranes, some significant drawbacks have been reported to limit their operational utilization. The main one is related to the fouling, in which operational cost increment and membrane lifetime alleviation are the consequence of this circumstance while affecting the water flux and salt rejection [5,6,8]. Although chlorination is deemed to be the potent approach for microbial deactivation and biofouling lowering, chlorine attack susceptibility of PA membranes with aromatic rings amide nitrogen is another drawback, since the flux alteration is followed because of the PA chain flexibility increment and hydrogen bonding change. Water passage restriction through the membrane was reported after chlorination at high pH [9–12].

The investigations regarding the improvement of chlorine-resistance and antifouling of membranes have become urgent for researchers to develop novel TFC membranes and diverse methods, including surface modification [13,14], substrate modification [15,16], different monomer utilization [17,18], and changing the polymerization parameters [19,20]. Meanwhile, within the mentioned methods, the incorporation of nanoparticles (NPs) was found to be an effective and economical approach toward membrane modification since they are used in a small amount and have a large surface area. On the other hand, the change of separation mechanism from diffusion to size-exclusion, improving the permeability-selectivity trade-off, and biofouling resistance, due to the anti-microorganism properties, were proved as the remarkable consequence of nanoparticle incorporation [6,21]. In this context, numerous nanomaterials such as covalent organic framework (COF) [22] zeolite [23], Ag [24], Al2O3 [25], CNT [26], GO [27], TiO2 [28], SiO2 [29], and CeO2 [30] have been investigated in the TFC membranes modification.

Among the mentioned nanomaterials, TiO2 is very important due to its excellent stability in acidic environments and high temperature, high hydrophilicity, and mechanical resistance. According to the investigation proposed by Shafiq et al., high chlorine resistance and salt rejection with great antibacterial activity were obtained by incorporating TiO2 [31]. Meanwhile, the semiconducting behavior of TiO2, which is stemmed from the bandgap of around 3.2 eV and the absorption wavelength of about 400 nm, makes it a promising material in the heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation of water pollutants [32]. However, the agglomeration and insufficient solar energy absorption observed in TiO2 are important limitations, which can be repressed by coupling with other materials [33,34]. Among the diverse materials, carbon-based materials are supposed to be propitious for coupling with TiO2 [35].

Although different types of carbon-containing materials including graphene, graphene oxide (GO), and carbon nanotube (CNT) have been evaluated in membrane modification, carbon dots (CDs) are highly regarded due to their low toxicity, ease of fabrication and functionalization, and excellent water dispersibility, because of the functional groups present in their structure. Simultaneously, hydrophilicity is beneficial to enhance the hydrophilicity of membranes [36]. These materials are also favorable for coupling to different semiconductors to enhance the photocatalytic performance through providing more sites for adsorption of pollutants and charge separation centers for recombination impeding and extending the electron/hole lifetime [35]. At the same time, it was supposed that CDs could be effective in improving chlorine-resistance performance [37–39]. On the other hand, CDs encapsulation is urgent for controlling their leaching and inhibition from the membrane performance deterioration [40]. Based on these explanations, in this work, modification of the TFC membrane with TiO2/CDs was evaluated. It seems that TiO2 coupling with CDs is a practical way to overcome the mentioned bottleneck of TiO2, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance, water flux, and salt rejection.

Fresh distilled water was used in all processes of this study. Also, no purification has been done on the materials used. TiO2 P25 nanoparticles (99.5%) were purchased from Degussa (Evonic). The citric acid (99%) and urea (99%) were prepared from Merck. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent and polysulfone polymer (PSf Ultrason S6010) were applied to make asymmetric porous substrates of TFC membranes on a polyester non-woven fabric. Also, in order to form a PA layer on the PSf support, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), n-hexane, trimesoyl chloride (TMC), (+) 10-camphor sulfonic acid (CSA), and triethylamine (TEA) were acquired from Merck Co., Germany. In order to investigate the antifouling properties, chlorine resistance performance, and rejection of membranes, NaCl, NaClO solution (10% w/v) (prepared by Merck Co., Germany), and humic acid (HA, prepared by Sigma Aldrich Co., Germany) were applied.

In order to preparation CDs, first 3.0 g (4.9 mmol) of urea and 3.0 g (1.5 mmol) of citric acid were added to 10 mL of water. The clear solution was irradiated in a domestic 750 W microwave oven for 5 min. At this point, the colorless liquid solution changed to a brown solid. It was then heated in an oven at 60 °C for 1 h to remove the volatile molecules. Then, the resulting powder was dispersed in 180 mL of water and finally centrifuged to remove agglomerated CDs [41]. In summary, for preparing TiO2/CDs, 0.4 g of TiO2 (5 mmol) and 0.5 g of CDs suspension in 100 mL of water were ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min. Eventually, the suspension was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 16 h.

The asymmetric PSf porous substrate, similar to our previous work, was synthesized using the phase inversion method (NIPS) and casting solution containing 81 wt% NMP as a solvent and 19 wt% PSf polymer [42].

RO membrane was formed using interfacial polymerization of aqueous MPD and organic phase TMC monomers on a porous PSf substrate. In order to perform the interfacial polymerization process, the following steps were performed [43]. First, the PSf substrate membrane was kept in distilled water for 30 min. Then, it was taken out of the water and contacted with MPD, TEA, and CSA (all 2 wt%) as an aqueous solution. This contact caused the solution to penetrate into the porous support pores. In the next step, the excess MPD solution was drained, and a rolling rubber roller was used to remove the remaining MPD droplets on the top surface of the PSf substrate. This was done until the MPD droplets were removed entirely. Then, TMC/n-hexane 0.1 wt%, as organic phase, was contacted to the upper surface of the PSf membrane. After 1 min, the remaining TMC/n-hexane solution was removed, and the entire membrane surface was thoroughly washed using n-hexane solution to completely remove the remaining agents. Finally, the membrane was cured at 70 °C for 10 min. For fabrication of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, TiO2/CDs nanocomposite was dispersed in water by a probe sonicator (Bandelin UW 3200, Germany) at ambient temperature for 7 min and next, the MPD, TEA and CSA were added. The modified nanocomposite membranes contained 0.001 to 0.02 wt% TiO2/CDs nanocomposite and included 2 wt% TEA and CSA agents (relative to the total weight of MPD solution) in the MPD solution.

EDX spectrum and FESEM image of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite were obtained using a TESCAN microscope. Textural properties were investigated using the BELSORP MINI II N2 adsorption apparatus. Perkin Elmer instrument was also applied to determine the functional groups of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite. The phase structure of TiO2/CDs was also determined by a diffractometer under Cu-Kα radiation (Philips Xpert). The optical properties of the nanoparticle were analyzed by a Scinco 4100 UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN, Czech Republic) was used to assess the morphology of the surface and cross-section of the membranes. For SEM samples preparation, they were coated with a thin layer of gold nanoparticles before measurement. To determine the presence and good dispersion of the TiO2/CDs nanoparticle in the PA layer matrix, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was applied.

Surface roughness parameters and membrane morphology were analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dual Scope C-26, DME Corp., Denmark). For this purpose, dry membrane samples with a specified area were installed on the holder and were scanned by tapping in the air. Also, surpass solid surface zeta potential (Anton Paar EKA) device was used to measure the surface charge of membranes. For this purpose, the membranes were pre-washed with deionized water. Then, KCl solution with a concentration of 0.001 M was used for the measurements. Also, using 0.1 M NaOH and HCl solutions, the pH of the solutions was adjusted in the range of 3–10. Finally, using Eq. (1), the zeta potential values were calculated.
(1)

in the above equation, ξ is the zeta potential. Also, L, η, R, Q, ε, ε
̥ and dU/dp are system channels length, electrolyte solution viscosity, Ac resistance of the cell using the electrolyte solution, cross-section of the channels, electrolyte dielectric constant, power and streaming potential slope against pressure, respectively.

Meanwhile, to evaluate the hydrophilicity of the modified membranes, the contact angle of the water (at rest) was measured using a sessile drop method by goniometer (G10, Kruss, Germany). This experiment was performed at 25 °C, and to reduce the laboratory error, 10 measurements were repeated in different places, and the results were reported as average.

The performance of TiO2/CDs RO membranes was investigated by a cross-flow filtration system consisting of three filtration cells (made of stainless steel 316) with an effective surface area of 36 cm2. This process was performed at ambient temperature and pressure of 1.5 MPa. At least four different membrane sheets were measured for better evaluation of the TFC-RO membranes' performance, and the results were reported as average. For this purpose, first using pure water, all membranes were compacted in 2.0 MPa for 30 min for fast reaching to the stabilized flux. Then, at a pressure of 1.5 MPa for 90 min, the pure water flux of the membranes was measured.

By measuring the volume of permeate at regular intervals and using the following equation, the permeate water flux of the membranes was calculated.
(2)
J =
Q At
in this equation, J, Q, and A are permeate water flux (L/m2·h), permeate volume (L) at time interval t, and effective membrane area (m2), respectively.

Additionally, 2000 mg/L NaCl solution was used to evaluate the performance of TFC-RO membranes after pure water filtration at 1.5 MPa for 90 min. Then, the concentration difference between feed and permeate solution was measured using a conductometer, and the salt rejection rate (S.R. (%)) of the membranes was calculated according to Eq. (3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling feature of the TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Toward investigating the antifouling feature of the manufactured RO membrane, 100 mg/L solution of HA, as an organic foulant model due to the strong fouling effect, was filtered. For this reason, first, the bare and the modified membranes were compacted until reaching the constant flux by pure water (at a pressure of 2.0 MPa for 30 min). Then, the pure water was substituted by HA solution, and the filtration experiment with HA solution at 1.5 MPa for 12 h was done. The fouling properties of fabricated membranes were assessed based on the flux decline of the bare and modified membranes under the filtration of HA solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of UV irradiation on flux recovery ratio
ce:section.ce:para:
UV irradiation was performed during membrane washing after fouling with HA/NaCl solution during 2 h filtration test to show the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2/CDs on the improvement of antifouling properties of the RO nanocomposite membranes. UV light (40 W) was applied for 10 min during membrane washing with a distance of 10 cm from the membrane surface when the membrane was immersed in the distilled water. After washing with distilled water, the second pure water flux experiment was conducted for 90 min. Then, the flux recovery ratio (FRR) after UV irradiation was calculated using Eq. (4) and compared with no UV irradiation results.
(4)
FRR =
J R
J 0
× 100
The fouling parameters i.e. reversible and irreversible fouling ratios were calculated based on our previous work [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chlorine resistance feature of the TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The resistance of membranes against the chlorine was explored by short-time contact of the manufactured membranes to the high amount of free chlorine because the destructive effect of short-time contact of membranes to the high chlorine amounts is the same as long-time contact of membranes to the low chlorine amounts [42]. For this purpose, by diluting the hypochlorite solution in the distilled water, the chlorine solution with a 1000 mg/L concentration was prepared, and it was used as a feed solution for the RO process. Due to the more destructive and higher oxidation behavior of chlorine solution in the pH below 7, the pH value of the chlorine solution was fixed on 4.0 by the 0.1 M solution of HCl. The chlorine resistance test of the fabricated membranes was evaluated by a three-step experiment. In the first stage, the performance of the bare and the modified membranes was assessed by NaCl solution with a 2000 mg/L concentration. Then, after the completion NaCl filtration test, the membranes were washed with distilled water; subsequently, the chlorine solution was filtered at pH = 4 and ambient temperature for 2 h. Finally, at the last step, the TFC and TFN membranes were washed with distilled water, and the performance of the membranes was again assessed by 2000 mg/L of NaCl solution.

XRD analysis was utilized to identify the phase purity and crystal structure of the TiO2/CDs sample. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the XRD pattern is compatible with the tetragonal phase of TiO2 (JCPDS card no. 04- 0477) [32]. Diffraction peaks of carbon dots are absent in the pattern of TiO2/CDs due to the low content of CDs in the fabricated materials [35]. This approved that most of the TiO2/CDs nanoparticles are made from TiO2, and the CDs are probably located within these TiO2 to improve the dispersibility of the resulting NPs in organic solvents. To investigate the presence of desired elements in the TiO2/CDs sample, EDX analysis was performed, as shown in Fig. 1b. The spectrum proves the existence of selected elements (Ti, C, and O), which demonstrates that CDs have been successfully anchored on the TiO2 with a low amount. The chemical structure of the prepared TiO2/CDs sample was probed through FT-IR measurements. As it can be observed in Fig. 1c, the bands at wavenumbers of 3300–3500 and 500–800 cm−1 are related to the OH and Ti-O-Ti groups, respectively [34]. Moreover, the bands located at 1440 and 1630 cm−1 are well-matched with the stretching vibrations of CO and CC groups from the CDs, respectively [44]. The FESEM image of the TiO2/CDs sample in Fig. 1d represents the spherical structure with an average size of about 30 nm. In addition, the N2 sorption technique was employed to evaluate the detailed porosity of the TiO2/CDs sample. As shown in Fig. 1e, the TiO2/CDs sample represents a type-II isotherm with an H3 hysteresis loop, which displays the mesoporous characteristics. The total pore volume, surface area, and average pore diameter of this sample are found to be 0.1356 cm3/g, 62.84 m2/g, and 8.6 nm, respectively. The optical properties of the synthesized pure TiO2 and TiO2/CDs were investigated by UV–vis DRS analysis (Fig. 1f). Comparison of the obtained TiO2/CDs spectrum by the pure TiO2 spectrum revealed the improved adsorption and the ability of TiO2/CDs for absorption in the visible region. A weak peak appears in 400–500 nm area. This could increase the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 nanoparticles [45,46].

The morphology of TiO2/CDs RO membranes was investigated using SEM analysis. The results are shown in Fig. 2 . According to the images, it was observed that the PA layer has a non-uniform and uneven surface with many strong knots. The ridge-and-valley structure is known as the typical TFC (MPD/TMC polyamide) membrane morphology [47], except that it is much denser and the grain structure is much more dispersed. Also, according to SEM images of the membrane surface, the membrane surface softened with increasing concentration of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite. This may be due to the formation of a hydrogen bond between the functional groups of hydrophilic TiO2/CDs nanocomposite and the PA layer [48]. In addition, since TiO2/CDs nanocomposite has functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl, they decreased the surface roughness of the membranes by increasing hydrophilicity [49,50]. This incident will be discussed in detail in the next section.

Cross-sectional images of the membrane containing 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs with three magnifications are shown in Fig. 3 , and the asymmetric structure of the PSf substrate is well-formed. In addition, a dense PA layer formed on the upper surface of the substrate.

The EDX-mapping analysis was conducted for evaluating the incorporation of TiO2/CDs into the PA layer and also showing the good distribution of them as presented Fig. 4 . It can be seen from the mapping of Ti that TiO2/CDs nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed in the PA layer of the RO membranes, and with the increase of the NPs amount in the MPD aqueous solution, the content of Ti dots is increased in the EDX-mapping image.

AFM, zeta potential, and water contact angle analyzes were performed in the following sections to investigate the effect of TiO2/CDs on membrane properties further. By using AFM analysis, the surface roughness parameters and morphology of TFC-RO membranes were examined at a scan size of 5 μm × 5 μm. As can be seen from the 3D images presented in Fig. 5 , the surface of the membranes is softened by the addition of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite to the PA layer. The surface roughness parameters of TFC-RO membranes are shown in Table 1 . The S y, S a, S q, and S z represent the mean square root of z, mean roughness, the difference between the highest peak and the lowest valley, and the highest peak, respectively. According to the results (Table 1), the average roughness (S a) of all TiO2/CDs modified membranes was lower than the pure membrane and gradually decreased with increasing the nanocomposite content. As mentioned in the SEM image review, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the TiO2/CDs nanocomposite and the PA layer can reduce the surface roughness of the membranes [42]. Probably, by the addition of hydrophilic nanoparticles and formation of H-bridges between the nanoparticles MPD/water solution, the diffusion rate of MPD on the TMC/hexane solution reduces, and the produced low height nodules causes the roughness reduction [51,52]. On the other hand, according to the results obtained from the contact angle, the addition of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite can reduce the interface energy, thus facilitating the migration of nanoparticles to the membrane surface. This induces hydrophilicity in the membrane, which ultimately leads to increased flux and more water absorption by the membrane [22]. Also, a decrease in contact angle and increase in hydrophilicity can be ascribed to oxygen-containing functional groups with a negative charge of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite. Therefore, by reducing the roughness and increasing the hydrophilicity of the surface, the adhesion and trapping of contaminants or proteins in the peaks and valleys of the surface of the modified membranes are reduced. Therefore, a membrane with a smooth surface has less fouling compared to a membrane with a rough surface so that it will have a higher flux rate [53]. Also, the trend of changing in the average roughness parameter was confirmed with the results obtained from the antifouling properties of the membranes. Therefore, surface roughness is one of the effective parameters of membrane fouling because it can affect the absorption and excretion of materials on the membrane surface. The membrane containing 0.02 wt% TiO2/CDs nanocomposite presented improved roughness, despite having the lowest contact angle. This may be due to the condensation of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite in high amounts, which has increased the surface roughness of the membrane [54,55].

The contact angle of water is one of the critical parameters for evaluating the hydrophilicity of membranes. Owing to this fact, the hydrophilicity of the bare TFC and TFN nanocomposite RO membranes was measured in the static mode, and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . As can be observed, the pristine TFC-RO membrane has the highest contact angle with water that shows the higher hydrophobicity of this membrane. On the other hand, by loading the TiO2/CDs nanocomposite in the PA layer, the water contact angle was reduced so that the membrane contained the highest amount of the TiO2/CDs showed the lowest contact angle and the most hydrophilicity. This change in water contact angle can be attributed to the high hydrophilic properties of TiO2/CD nanocomposite caused by the existence of hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl in the structure of TiO2/CD nanocomposite [56–58].

In RO membranes, the surface charge of the membrane is owing to the Donnan mechanism. This mechanism works based on electrostatic repulsive so that membranes with negative surface charge reject the pollutants with the negative charge like chlorine, anionic dyes, and salts [59]. On the other hand, the membrane fouling by sedimentation of pollutants in the membrane surface is the main disadvantage of membrane filtration [60]. The membrane with a negative surface charge has a good antifouling feature because the sedimentation of negative pollutants was prevented by electrostatic repulsive interaction. The outcomes of the zeta potential in the pH range from 3 to 10 of the bare and 0.01 wt% nanocomposite membranes are shown in Fig. 7 . It was found that in natural pH, the bare and the modified membranes have a negative surface charge, so this can affect the rejection performance of the fabricated membrane. Thanks to the existence of negative functional groups in the structure of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite, the embedded TFN-RO membrane had a more negative surface charge. Also, the hydrolysis of TMC to acyl chloride can be the reason for the negative charge of the bare membrane [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desalination performance of TiO2/CDs RO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8a shows the results of pure water flux with NaCl solution flux, and Fig. 8b shows the rejection of NaCl solution. This step was performed to investigate the desalination performance of the modified TiO2/CDs RO membranes and the bare membrane. All modified membranes showed higher pure water fluxes than the bare membranes. By increasing the amount of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite to the optimum value of 0.01 wt%, the pure water flux increased (from 40.5 to 59.6 L/m2·h). This increase in permeability is due to the hydrophilic functional groups in TiO2/CDs nanocomposite, which are located near the membrane surface, and also the high tendency of TiO2 nanoparticles to be hydrolyzed with hydroxyl groups to induce more hydrophilicity to the membrane [61]. In addition, proper dispersion of the TiO2 nanoparticle due to the presence of CDs reduces agglomeration and causes better dispersion of the nanocomposite in the PA layer, and increases the flux. However, at higher values (0.02 wt%), despite the higher hydrophilicity of the membrane surface, the membrane flux decreased. This may be due to the pore blockage at high concentrations of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite. Also, according to AFM images, the accumulation of nanoparticles and their improper dispersion in the PA layer increased the roughness of the membrane surface and decreased the membrane flux. Fig. 8b shows the rejection of the NaCl solution. Despite the high flux, an increase in salt retention was observed. This upward trend is observed in the increment of regression due to the more negative charge of the membrane surface. A comparison between the results of permeability and rejection can point to the excellent performance of the membranes. The upward trend observed in the improvement of permeability and rejection of modified membranes showed that this performance improvement is not derived from membrane defects, while it was created by the addition of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite. In fact, there is no exchange between salt rejection and membrane permeability. The observed reduction in salt rejection in membrane containing 0.02 wt% can be due to the accumulation of the nanocomposite in the PA layer of the membrane and the formation of membrane defects [62,63]. Finally, the placement of TiO2 nanoparticles on CDs prevents them from condensing on the membrane substrate and improves the separation performance of the modified membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling behavior of the TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fouling is a major problem for membranes in industrial applications. Therefore, the antifouling properties of the membrane must be improved. The outcomes of the antifouling experiment of the bare and the modified membranes are displayed in Fig. 9a. As can be observed, the bare TFC membrane had the highest, about 40%, flux decline, while the modified TFN membrane containing 0.02 wt% TiO2/CDs nanocomposite had the lowest flux decline (about 10%). Also, all of the modified membranes had a better antifouling performance. The improvement of membrane antifouling feature is because of electrostatic repulsion, the smoother surface, and more hydrophilicity of TiO2/CDs-TFN membranes due to the presence of NPs in the selective layer [64–66]. When the membrane surface becomes more hydrophilic, not only the hydrophobic pollutants due to hydrophobic interaction are repelled from the membrane surface, but also more water passes through the membrane. Moreover, as can be shown in Table 1, the membrane surface has become smoother. For this reason, less deposition of the contaminant on the membrane surface could happen, and with good mixing of the feed solution, the pollutants could be easily detached from the surface. Ultimately, the introduction of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite in the PA layer provides a more negative charge of modified membrane surface (see Fig. 7), so the electrostatic repulsion between the membrane and HA is increased, and the antifouling properties are improved consequently.

The photocatalytic activity of TiO2/CDs on the antifouling feature of TFN membranes was evaluated. The obtained results of FRR under the UV light during washing of membranes are shown in Fig. 9b. It is clear that the FRR value of all membranes after UV light treatment was increased. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2/CD nanocomposites has been confirmed by earlier studies [67,68]. Under UV light exposure, TiO2/CDs nanocomposite introduces degradation of HA attached to the membrane surface through the generated holes and hydroxyl radicals. In particular, hydroxyl radicals, hydrated electrons, and other oxidant radicals can be generated by exposing TiO2/CDs blended membrane surface to UV radiation, causing the attached HA to be destroyed and removed from the membrane surface. Also, under UV radiation, large HA is broken down into small molecular acids. Therefore, it is easier to remove the particles attached to the membrane surface by washing. When the TiO2/CD photocatalyst is exposed to UV light, the functional groups on the TiO2 can be excited to generate hole (h+) and photogenic electrons (e−). Then, photogenerated electrons (e−) were transferred from the valence band to the conduction band, and the hole (h+) remained in the valence band. The presence of CDs coupled with TiO2 leads to the capture of generated electrons and inhibition of electron-hole recombination. After the generation of electrons and holes, the electron can produce O2– radicals due to reacting with O2. On the other hand, holes can produce OH radicals due to a direct reaction with H2O. Consequently, the HA foulant was attacked by generated radicals and destroyed [69–71]. Therefore, the attached foulants can be degraded and easily removed by washing from the membrane surface. This claim was confirmed by the calculation of reversible and irreversible fouling parameters after usages of UV light assistance. As can be seen in Fig. 9c, the irreversible fouling after UV was reduced, while the reversible fouling enhancement, so this variation in resistance parameters indicates the changing of the membrane surface properties with UV irradiation. Consequently, the antifouling feature of TFN nanocomposite membranes improves. Also, the benefits of the usage of the photocatalytic properties for membranes cleaning and improvement of antifouling features were reported by other studies [33,72]. A slight increment in the FRR value of the bare TFC membrane without TiO2/CD nanocomposite may be due to the degradation of HA under UV light. In a more detailed study of FRR with and without UV radiation, in the pure membrane, 0.001 wt% TiO2/CDs membrane and 0.02 wt% TiO2/CDs membrane, different trends were observed than other membranes. In pure membrane, due to lack of active sites and in 0.001 wt% TiO2/CDs membrane due to low nanoparticle content and number of active sites, the photocatalytic activity is not well observed, and these differences were observed. While in the membrane 0.02 wt% TiO2/CDs, the low photocatalytic activity were occurred due to agglomeration of the nanoparticles in high amounts and reduction of the number of active sites due to blockage [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chlorine resistance of TiO2/CDs RO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The results of membrane salt rejection, after and before chlorination of TiO2/CDs RO membranes in sodium hypochlorite, are shown in Fig. 10a. It was observed that despite the decrease in the rate of rejection of NaCl solution after chlorination, the rejection trend is increasing. According to the results of zeta potential analysis and the more negative surface charge of membranes, chlorine resistance increased. The electrostatic repulsion between chlorine and the membrane surface reduces the possibility of chlorine's existence on the PA surface by decomposing the chlorine and increasing chlorine resistance [31]. Also, according to the results before and after chlorination, the rate of bare TFC-RO membrane rejection decreased from 97.5% to 78.5%. This decrease is due to the deformation of the PA chains. Because ring-chlorination reactions and subsequent N-chlorination cause the loss of intermolecular hydrogen bond and the symmetry of the PA lattice and finally change the crystalline phase to the amorphous phase. Also, this phenomenon can be theorized by the increment in flexibility and free volume of the polymer matrix, which is caused by the destruction of the PA layer [73]. For the modified TiO2/CDs RO membranes, a decrease in rejection performance was also observed, while the optimal membrane 0.01 wt% of TiO2/CDs showed a reduction of less than 1.5%. This could be due to the enhanced hydrophilicity of TiO2/CDs RO membranes, which acted as a barrier to protect the PA layer [74]. On the other hand, the chemical interaction between the active layer of the membrane and TiO2/CDs containing different functional groups increases the intermolecular hydrogen bond. This interaction increased the chlorine resistance of the membranes because it disrupted the replacement of hydrogen with chlorine in the membrane amide groups [62]. The existence of TiO2/CD in the active layer provides more sites for the chlorine attack and prevents damage to PA. Additionally, as mentioned earlier, the modified TFN membranes have a negative surface charge. Therefore, the contact between the PA active layer and chlorine in these membranes as well as damage of the PA layer is minimum.

Moreover, the effect of chlorination on water permeability was evaluated by measuring water flux after the chlorination. The results are shown in Fig. 10b. After chlorination, the water flux of all membranes was increased. When the membranes were exposed to the chlorine solution, the PA layer was damaged due to the oxidation ability of chlorine, so the dense structure and cross-link network of some PA was broken. As a result, more space for water passing was created, leading to an increase in the water flux [75,76]. In addition, the chlorination may break the hydrogen band of the PA layer that leads to forming a new pathway for water transfer [77]. Quantitatively, the water flux of the bare TFC after chlorination had a 30% enhancement, while this for 0.01 wt% TiO2/CDs membrane was only 2.5%. This indicated that the presence of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite in the PA active layer led to an increase in chlorine resistance of RO membranes. The existence of TiO2/CDs in the active layer provided more sites for the chlorine attack and prevented damage to PA.

Table 2 was prepared to make a brief comparison between our results with previous studies. It is clear that embedding of NPs in the selective layer of TFC and TFN-RO membranes led to high membrane performance. For instance, the embedding of the covalent organic framework (COF) in the PA selective layer leads to excellent chlorine resistance [87], while the metal-organic framework (MOF) leads to high separation efficiency [89]. Also, the modification of the PA layer by functionalized boron nitride nanosheets with amine groups led to the improvement of chlorine resistance [90]. The modification of RO membranes with nanostructures not only leads to enhancement of chlorine resistance but also leads to improvement of water flux, antibacterial feature, fouling resistance, desalination properties, and scaling reduction of RO membranes. Without considering the various types of nanoparticles, including organic, inorganic, carbon bases and etc., it was found that using any kind of nanomaterial causes improvement of membrane performance. Therefore, using a TFN membrane is a promising approach for the modification of the RO membrane.

In this study, the TiO2/CD nanocomposite was synthesized and applied for modification of the thin-film composite RO membrane. Through the characterization and morphology investigation, the results showed that the membranes containing TiO2/CDs nanocomposite had more hydrophilicity, smoother surface, and negative surface charge. Also, the effect of TiO2/CDs loading in the selective layer on the membrane performance was assessed in terms of pure water flux, NaCl salt rejection, flux decline, and resistance against chlorine. The membrane containing 0.01 wt% of TiO2/CDs nanocomposite indicated the highest pure water flux and salt rejection (59.6 L/m2.h and 99.2% respectively), the lowest flux decline (12%), and excellent chlorine resistance. Consequently, the TiO2/CDs nanocomposite is a suitable nanofiller to enhance chlorine resistance for modifying TFC-RO membranes.
